content.text: Deploying a resource to Azure always requires specifying a region. That single decision affects latency for every user interaction, which services are available for your architecture, data residency and compliance, disaster recovery options, and the monthly bill. Migrating a deployed workload from one region to another typically requires redeployment from scratch — most Azure resource types cannot be moved between regions. Making the right choice before the first 'az group create' saves significant rework.
Region selection is an architecture decision, not a deployment detail. It belongs in the Architecture Decision Record (ADR) alongside technology choices, not in a runbook.

attrs.alt: Azure Region Selection Decision Framework: start with regulations, branch to Azure Government (FedRAMP/ITAR) or Azure China (PIPL), then select geography, then evaluate latency/service availability/pricing within the geography

content.text: The flowchart above captures the three-stage decision. Stage 1 filters by mandatory compliance requirements that override all other factors. Stage 2 identifies the geography (data residency boundary). Stage 3 selects the specific region within that geography based on latency, service availability, and pricing.

If your workload must meet FedRAMP High, DoD CC SRG IL4/IL5, CJIS, or ITAR requirements, the choice is forced: deploy to Azure Government. These certifications are not available in commercial Azure, regardless of which US region you choose. Attempting to certify a commercial Azure deployment for FedRAMP High will fail — the operational controls (US-persons-only staff access, screened employees) only exist in Azure Government.

If your application processes personal data of Chinese citizens and falls under China's PIPL (Personal Information Protection Law), data must remain in mainland China. This forces Azure China (21Vianet) as the deployment target. Standard commercial Azure regions — even if physically close to China (Southeast Asia/Singapore) — do not satisfy this requirement because data could be accessible outside China.

If neither of the above applies, commercial Azure is appropriate. Most compliance frameworks — GDPR, UK GDPR, Australian Privacy Act, HIPAA, PCI DSS, SOC 2 — are satisfied by commercial Azure regions in the appropriate geography.
Stage 2: Geography Selection for Data Residency

Once you have confirmed commercial Azure is appropriate, select the geography that satisfies your data residency requirements. A geography is a set of regions that share a compliance boundary — GRS replication stays within it, and Microsoft's EU Data Boundary commitment applies within the Europe geography.
If your users / data subjects are in...
Deploy per geography; use Front Door to route

When users span multiple geographies — an e-commerce platform with EU and US customers — deploy one regional stack per geography and route users to the nearest stack via Azure Front Door or Traffic Manager. Never consolidate multi-geography users into a single region that crosses a compliance boundary.
Stage 3: Region Selection Within a Geography

Network round-trip time (RTT) between a user and an Azure region grows with distance. Typical figures: a user in the same city as the region's datacentre sees <10 ms RTT; a user 1,000 miles away sees 20–40 ms; a user across an ocean sees 100–250 ms. These numbers matter most for interactive applications — database queries on every click, real-time collaboration, video conferencing. For batch processing or async workflows, latency is irrelevant.

# Measure latency from the current machine to multiple Azure regions
# Azure publishes publicly accessible latency test URLs per region

declare -A REGION_HOSTS=(
  ["eastus"]="eastus.latency.azure.com"
  ["westus2"]="westus2.latency.azure.com"
  ["northeurope"]="northeurope.latency.azure.com"
  ["westeurope"]="westeurope.latency.azure.com"
  ["uksouth"]="uksouth.latency.azure.com"
  ["australiaeast"]="australiaeast.latency.azure.com"
  ["japaneast"]="japaneast.latency.azure.com"
  ["southeastasia"]="southeastasia.latency.azure.com"
)

echo "Region                  RTT (s)"
echo "---------------------------------------"
for region in "${!REGION_HOSTS[@]}"; do
  host="${REGION_HOSTS[$region]}"
  rtt=$(curl -o /dev/null -s -w '%{time_total}' --max-time 5 "https://$host" 2>/dev/null)
  printf "%-25s %s\n" "$region" "${rtt:-timeout}"
done | sort -k2 -n

The Azure Speed Test (azurespeed.com) provides a browser-based version of this test, including upload and download throughput to each region — useful for validating media upload or large data transfer scenarios.

Not all Azure services are available in all regions. New services launch first in primary regions (East US, West Europe, Southeast Asia) and expand over 6–18 months. GPU VM families (NC/ND/NV-series), Azure OpenAI, Azure Container Apps, and preview features are particularly prone to limited regional availability.

# Check if ALL required services are available in your target region
# before committing to it in your architecture

TARGET_REGION="uksouth"

# 1. Check VM SKU availability (critical for GPU or high-memory workloads)
az vm list-skus \
  --location "$TARGET_REGION" \
  --size Standard_NC \
  --query "[].{SKU:name, Restrictions:restrictions[].reasonCode}" \
  --output table

# 2. Check if Azure OpenAI is available (important for AI workloads)
az cognitiveservices account list-kinds \
  --query "[?kind=='OpenAI']" \
  --output json

# 3. Check Kubernetes (AKS) version availability
az aks get-versions \
  --location "$TARGET_REGION" \
  --query "values[].version" \
  --output table

# 4. Check Cosmos DB API support
az cosmosdb list-locations \
  --query "[?name=='$TARGET_REGION'].{Name:name, Failover:failoverPriority}" \
  --output table

# 5. List all resource provider namespaces and their region status
az provider list \
  --query "[?registrationState=='Registered'].namespace" \
  --output tsv | head -20

The official 'Azure products available by region' page at azure.microsoft.com/en-us/explore/global-infrastructure/products-by-region/ provides a searchable matrix. For scripted validation, use the az provider and az vm list-skus commands above in your CI/CD pipeline to gate deployments to unsupported regions.

Azure prices are not uniform across regions. East US (Virginia) is generally the lowest-cost US region because it has the highest concentration of capacity. Newer regions and remote geographies carry 10–40% premiums on compute and storage compared to East US.
Highest capacity; lowest prices for most services
Higher infrastructure costs in SE Asia
Significant premium; energy and logistics costs
High land and power costs in Tokyo
Growing capacity; modest premium over US

# Compare VM pricing across regions using Azure Retail Prices API
python3 << 'EOF'
import urllib.request, json

url = ("https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices"
       "?api-version=2023-01-01-preview"
       "&$filter=serviceName eq 'Virtual Machines'"
       " and armSkuName eq 'Standard_D4s_v5'"
       " and priceType eq 'Consumption'"
       " and contains(productName,'Linux')")

with urllib.request.urlopen(url) as r:
    items = json.loads(r.read())["Items"]

price_by_region = {}
for item in items:
    if item.get("retailPrice", 0) > 0:
        price_by_region[item["armRegionName"]] = item["retailPrice"]

baseline = price_by_region.get("eastus", 1)
print(f"{'Region':<35} {'$/hr':>7}  {'vs East US':>10}")
print("-" * 58)
for region, price in sorted(price_by_region.items(), key=lambda x: x[1])[:15]:
    ratio = price / baseline
    print(f"{region:<35} {price:>7.4f}  {ratio:>9.2f}x")
EOF
Availability Zones: An Additional Region Filter

If your architecture requires zone-redundant services (AKS across AZs, ZRS storage, zone-pinned VMs, Standard Load Balancer zone-redundancy), you must deploy to a region that supports Availability Zones. Not all Azure regions have AZs — particularly newer or smaller regions.

# List all regions that support Availability Zones
az account list-locations \
  --query "[?not_null(availabilityZoneMappings) && length(availabilityZoneMappings) > '0'].{Region:name, Display:displayName, Zones:length(availabilityZoneMappings)}" \
  --output table | head -30

# Check specific region for AZ support
az account list-locations \
  --query "[?name=='uksouth'].availabilityZoneMappings[].logicalZone" \
  --output tsv
# Returns: 1, 2, 3 if AZs exist; empty if not supported

Major regions — East US, West US 2, North Europe, West Europe, UK South, Australia East, Japan East, Southeast Asia, and most others in the primary tier — support AZs. When choosing between two regions within the same geography, prefer the one with AZ support for production workloads.

Your primary region choice determines your DR secondary, because GRS and Azure Site Recovery use the fixed region pair. Before finalising a region, confirm the pair is also acceptable for your DR requirements:
East US pairs with West US — both in Americas; acceptable for US data.
North Europe (Ireland) pairs with West Europe (Netherlands) — both in EU geography; acceptable for GDPR.
UK South pairs with UK West — both in UK; acceptable for UK data sovereignty.
Australia East pairs with Australia Southeast — both in Australia; acceptable for Australian Privacy Act.
Brazil South pairs with Brazil Southeast (or historically South Central US — verify current pairing) — Brazil-only for LGPD compliance.
Multi-Region Deployments: Selecting the Full Set

Many production architectures use multiple regions: a primary for active traffic, a secondary for DR, and optionally additional regions for read replicas or CDN origin servers. The selection process extends naturally from the single-region framework:
Choose the primary region using the framework above (compliance → geography → latency/services/price).
The DR secondary is the region pair of your primary — confirm it satisfies the same compliance requirements.
For additional read regions (Cosmos DB multi-region, SQL Database geo-replication, CDN origins), select regions that minimise latency to remaining user populations not well-served by the primary.
Document all chosen regions in the ADR with their role (primary, DR, read replica, CDN origin) and the justification for each.

# Example: provision a 3-region Cosmos DB account
# Primary: East US (US users), Read replica: West Europe (EU users), DR: West US 2
az cosmosdb create \
  --name prod-cosmos-global \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --locations \
    regionName=eastus       failoverPriority=0 isZoneRedundant=true \
    regionName=westeurope   failoverPriority=1 isZoneRedundant=true \
    regionName=westus2      failoverPriority=2 isZoneRedundant=false \
  --default-consistency-level Session \
  --enable-multiple-write-locations false  # Single-write; failover is auto

# Confirm regions and failover priorities
az cosmosdb show \
  --name prod-cosmos-global \
  --resource-group prod-rg \
  --query "locations[].{Region:locationName, Priority:failoverPriority, ZR:isZoneRedundant}" \
  --output table
Region selection must be enforced in infrastructure-as-code, not left to individual engineers. Two layers of enforcement are recommended:
1. Variable validation in Terraform / parameter constraints in Bicep

# Azure Policy: deny resource creation outside approved EU regions
# Apply at Management Group level to cover all subscriptions

APPROVED='["northeurope","westeurope","uksouth","ukwest","germanywestcentral","germanynorth","francecentral","global"]'

az policy assignment create \
  --name "eu-geography-only" \
  --display-name "GDPR: Deny resources outside EU geography" \
  --policy "e56962a6-4747-49cd-b67b-bf8b01975c4c" \
  --scope "/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/<mg-id>" \
  --enforcement-mode Default \
  --params "{"listOfAllowedLocations":{"value":$APPROVED}}"

# Test what the policy would deny (use DoNotEnforce first to audit before enforcing)
az policy assignment create \
  --name "eu-geography-audit" \
  --policy "e56962a6-4747-49cd-b67b-bf8b01975c4c" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub-id>" \
  --enforcement-mode DoNotEnforce \   # Audit only — does not block deployments yet
  --params "{"listOfAllowedLocations":{"value":$APPROVED}}"

# Review compliance report after 24 hours
az policy state summarize \
  --policy-assignment "eu-geography-audit" \
  --query "results.nonCompliantResources" \
  --output json
Choosing the cheapest region without checking latency:

East US is cheapest for many services, but if all users are in Australia, 250 ms RTT from every API call compounds into a poor user experience. Price optimisation is a last resort, not a first filter.
Choosing a region before verifying all required services:
An architecture designed in East US that requires Azure OpenAI gpt-4o may fail to deploy in Australia East where availability lags. Verify service GA status before finalising the architecture.

A developer who picks UK South as primary without checking the pair (UK West) may later discover that UK West does not offer a service they assumed would be available for DR. Check the pair's service availability simultaneously with the primary.
Some regions support AZs; others do not. Designing a zone-redundant architecture and then deploying to a region without AZ support causes deployment failures at the zone assignment step.
Not locking the region in IaC from the start:

Teams that don't validate region in Terraform or Bicep at review time discover compliance violations months later during an audit. Policy enforcement plus IaC validation catches this at development time.
Treating 'global' as a region for compliance:

Some services (Azure CDN, Traffic Manager, Azure Active Directory) report location as 'global'. These must be explicitly excluded from Allowed Locations policy, and their actual data location must be assessed separately.
Compliance filter: Does the workload require FedRAMP High/ITAR? → Azure Government. Does it require China PIPL? → Azure China. Otherwise commercial Azure.
Geography: Identify the data subjects' jurisdiction and select the Azure geography whose region pair satisfies the applicable data protection law.
Latency: Run Azure Speed Test from representative user locations. Confirm RTT is within SLA for the most latency-sensitive operation in the application.
Service availability: Verify every service (including preview services and GPU SKUs) is GA in both the primary region and the DR pair region.
AZ support: Confirm the region supports Availability Zones if zone-redundant services are required.
Pricing: Compare the target region price against alternatives in the same geography using the Retail Prices API. Accept a higher-priced region only if latency or service availability requires it.
Region pair: Confirm the pair region satisfies the same compliance and service availability requirements — it becomes the DR target and GRS secondary.

IaC lock: Add a variable validation rule in Terraform or a parameter allowedValues constraint in Bicep to prevent deployment to any other region. Apply an Azure Policy Allowed Locations deny assignment at management group scope.
Document: Record the region, DR pair, geography, regulatory basis, and the latency/service/price rationale in the Architecture Decision Record.

After selecting a region, verify that the required VM SKU quotas exist before beginning deployment. Azure imposes per-subscription, per-region quotas on vCPU families. A large deployment (50+ VMs) to a new region will fail at scale if quotas have not been pre-provisioned.

# Check current quota usage and limits in the target region
az vm list-usage \
  --location northeurope \
  --query "[?name.value=='cores' || starts_with(name.value,'Standard D') || starts_with(name.value,'Standard NC')].{Family:name.localizedValue, Used:currentValue, Limit:limit}" \
  --output table

# Request a quota increase (submit a support ticket)
az support tickets create \
  --ticket-name "quota-northeurope-d4sv5" \
  --title "vCPU quota increase: Standard DSv5, North Europe" \
  --description "Requesting 200 vCPUs of Standard_DSv5 family in northeurope for Q3 production migration." \
  --problem-classification "/providers/Microsoft.Support/services/quota_service_id/problemClassifications/cores" \
  --severity minimal \
  --contact-first-name "Platform" \
  --contact-last-name "Team" \
  --contact-email "platform@contoso.com" \
  --contact-country "IE"

# For large deployments, request quota 2–4 weeks before the deployment date.
# Microsoft processes quota requests within 1–5 business days for standard requests.

# Check quota remaining for a specific VM family
az vm list-usage \
  --location northeurope \
  --query "[?name.value=='standardDSv5Family'].{Used:currentValue, Limit:limit, Available:"limit - currentValue"}" \
  --output json

Quota increase requests should be submitted as early as possible — ideally at architecture design time rather than the day before deployment. Include a justification describing the workload, expected ramp-up timeline, and peak instance count. Microsoft may request more detail for large quotas (>1,000 vCPUs).
Testing Region Choice Before Full Deployment
Before committing a full environment to a region, validate assumptions with a minimal proof-of-concept deployment that exercises the critical path:

Deploy a minimal VM or App Service in the candidate region, make API calls from representative user locations (use a VPN or cloud VM in the user's geography), and measure RTT on the actual stack — not just the Azure Speed Test ping.
Deploy each required service type (AKS, Cosmos DB, Azure OpenAI, specific VM SKUs) in the candidate region to confirm GA availability and expected API behaviour.
Run the Azure Pricing Calculator for the full architecture in the candidate region and in one alternative. If the premium exceeds 15%, revisit the latency-vs-cost tradeoff.
Run the Policy compliance check (DoNotEnforce assignment) in the candidate subscription and confirm zero non-compliant resources before enabling enforcement.
Ten hours of validation before a region commitment saves weeks of migration work later. Region changes in production require redeployment from scratch for most Azure resource types.

Region selection follows a three-stage process. First, compliance requirements may mandate a sovereign cloud: FedRAMP High and ITAR require Azure Government; China PIPL requires Azure China. For all other workloads, commercial Azure is appropriate. Second, choose the geography that satisfies your data residency regulation — EU geography for GDPR, UK South/West for UK GDPR, Australia East/SE for the Australian Privacy Act. Third, within that geography, evaluate the specific region on three dimensions: latency to end users (run the Azure Speed Test), service availability (verify every required service is GA in the target and its pair region), and pricing (East US is cheapest; AU/Brazil carry 20–40% premiums). Lock the chosen region in Terraform validation rules and Azure Policy before any resources are deployed — it is far cheaper to enforce at design time than to migrate a running workload later.
